content:
10 min read
1 hour ago

--

Full disclosure: I work in the engineering team at Juicer, so I’m not a neutral party. But this is a real, hands-on walkthrough, not a sales pitch.
In case you haven’t come across Juicer before, it’s a tool for aggregating social media. You connect the accounts you post from — Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, Facebook, X, and others — and Juicer gathers those posts into a single feed you can embed on your website. The point is to save you the work of integrating with each platform yourself. Every social network has its own API, its own login flow, and its own habit of changing things without warning, and keeping up with all of them is a real chore. Juicer does that part for you, so a feed you set up once stays current on its own.
Setting one up has always been a matter of clicking through the Juicer dashboard. This post covers the other way to do it: building the same feed through our API, from creating it to getting the embed code, all in a handful of requests.
The API is worth reaching for when the dashboard starts to feel repetitive:

• You’re doing it more than once — spinning up a feed per customer, per campaign, or per landing page.

• You want it in your own product — let your users assemble a feed without ever seeing Juicer’s UI.

• You want it to stay in sync with something else — a CMS, a list of brands, a spreadsheet of handles.
If you just need one feed once, the dashboard is faster. If any of the above sound like you, read on. Everything below is curl; it's the same key and the same JSON you'll use for every other post in this series.
Two words first: feed and source
If you’re new to Juicer, the whole model is two nouns:

• A source is one stream of posts from one place — a single handle, hashtag, channel, or subreddit on a single network. @NASA on YouTube is a source; #travel on Instagram is another. Each is defined by a platform, a term, and a term_type (is that term a username, a hashtag, a channel…?).

• A feed is the container that blends many sources into one stream and renders them as a single wall. It’s the thing you embed on your site; a source is never embedded on its own.
That’s the entire aggregation idea: many sources in, one feed out. The rest of this post is just creating a feed, pouring sources into it, and grabbing the embed code — which is also, not by coincidence, the exact order of the API calls.
Step 0: get a key
Every request to the API carries a key in an Authorization: Bearer header, so before anything else you need one. We built the API around a low barrier to start: the goal was that you can be making real calls within a minute of deciding to try it — no sales call, no credit card, and no "create an account, verify your email, pick a plan, find the API section" gauntlet before you've written a single request.
There are two ways to get a key, and you can pick based on where you are:
If you already have a Juicer account, grab one from the Developer page in the dashboard — create a key, copy it, done:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you don’t — or you just want to start right now — you don’t need to sign up first. Ask for a key with nothing but an email:
curl -X POST https://api.juicer.io/v1/authorize \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-d '{"email": "you@example.com"}'For a new email, the key comes back in that same response — no dashboard, no password, no credit card:
{
"status": "issued",
"api_key": "jcr_9f...ab",
"expires_at": "2026-07-04T12:00:00Z",
"email_confirmation_required": true,
"rate_limit": { "requests_per_hour": 60 },
"upgrade_hint": "Confirm your email to raise your rate limit to 300 requests/hour and extend this key to 12 hours."
}Juicer quietly provisions an account for that email behind the scenes, so you get the key first and finish setting up the account later. The temporary key works immediately — enough to build and test the whole feed in this post — capped at 60 requests/hour for a couple of hours. When you’re ready, confirming the email (Juicer sends the link) lifts you to 300 requests/hour and extends the key. It’s genuinely “try first, commit later.” (If the email already belongs to a confirmed Juicer user, you’ll instead get a quick one-time browser approval step — the response tells you which path you’re on.)
Either way, stash the key so the rest of the calls stay tidy:
export JCR_KEY="jcr_9f...ab" # the api_key from the responseStep 1: create the feed
A feed is just a named container to start. Sources come next.
curl -X POST https://api.juicer.io/v1/feeds \
-H "Authorization: Bearer $JCR_KEY" \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-d '{"name": "Our Brand Wall"}'{
"data": {
"id": 84213,
"name": "Our Brand Wall",
"slug": "our-brand-wall",
"sources_count": 0,
"created_at": "2026-07-04T10:00:00Z"
}
}Keep two things from that response: the id (for adding sources) and the slug (it shows up in the embed). If your plan's feed limit is maxed, you'll get a 422 with error.code = "feed_limit_reached" and an upgrade link instead — worth handling if you're provisioning feeds programmatically.
Step 2: add your first source
Adding a source takes three fields — platform, term, and term_type — and creates the source and kicks off an initial sync to pull posts. Let's start with YouTube, which needs no connection at all — you can point it at any public channel — so we get a quick win:
curl -X POST https://api.juicer.io/v1/feeds/84213/sources \
-H "Authorization: Bearer $JCR_KEY" \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-d '{"platform": "YouTube", "term": "NASA", "term_type": "username"}'{
"data": {
"id": 550001,
"platform": "YouTube",
"term": "NASA",
"term_type": "username",
"name": null,
"options": {},
"syncable": true,
"unsyncable_reason": null,
"synced_at": "2026-07-04T10:01:02Z",
"created_at": "2026-07-04T10:01:00Z"
}
}syncable: true means the source is good and its first sync has run. If a source can't sync, syncable comes back false with a human-readable unsyncable_reason. Check those rather than assuming every source is instantly full of posts.
Don’t guess term types — discover them
Each platform accepts different term_type values (username, hashtag, channel, mentions…). Rather than hardcode them, ask the API:
curl https://api.juicer.io/v1/platforms \
-H "Authorization: Bearer $JCR_KEY"Each platform lists its term_types, each with a display_name — and, crucially, a requires_connection object when that term type needs a connected social account. That field is the whole subject of the next step.
Step 3: connect your accounts
YouTube let us point a source at any public channel with nothing but its name. Several networks aren’t so open: Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, and X only expose their posts to a connected account — and for a username source, only an account you actually control. So on these platforms you're aggregating your own accounts, not arbitrary handles. Try adding Instagram (use your own handle here) and you'll see the difference:
curl -X POST https://api.juicer.io/v1/feeds/84213/sources \
-H "Authorization: Bearer $JCR_KEY" \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-d '{"platform": "Instagram", "term": "yourbrand", "term_type": "username"}'Instead of a 201, you get a 422 that tells you what's missing — and hands you the fix:
{
"error": {
"code": "social_account_required",
"message": "Requires the specific Instagram account to be connected. The connected account name must match the term.",
"action": {
"type": "connect_social_account",
"provider": "instagram_login",
"connection_url": "https://www.juicer.io/auth/911063ea...",
"expires_at": "2026-07-04T10:35:00Z"
}
}
}This isn’t a failure on your part — it’s the API telling you to connect the account first, and the useful part is action.connection_url. It's a ready-to-use, single-use link (good for 30 minutes) you don't have to construct. Opening it in a browser signs the user in and drops them straight onto the platform's OAuth page — they don't even need to be logged into Juicer first. That's exactly how you'd let someone connect their account to a feed you're building for them: show a "Connect Instagram" button pointing at that URL.
You can also mint the same link up front, before hitting the error, with a dedicated call — handy if you’d rather prompt for connections proactively:
curl -X POST https://api.juicer.io/v1/social_accounts/connect_url \
-H "Authorization: Bearer $JCR_KEY" \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-d '{"provider": "instagram_login"}'{
"data": {
"provider": "instagram_login",
"connection_url": "https://www.juicer.io/auth/911063ea...",
"expires_at": "2026-07-04T10:35:00Z",
"allows_multiple": true,
"note": "Open this URL in a browser. It will sign you in and redirect to instagram_login authorization. The link expires in 30 minutes."
}
}Once the account is connected, confirm it landed with GET https://api.juicer.io/v1/social_accounts/status — it lists every provider and whether it's connected — then re-run the source creation from the top of this step. It returns 201 now.
Step 4: add the remaining networks
Here’s the part the “one feed, every network” pitch tends to gloss over: each login-gated network is its own connection. Connecting Instagram in Step 3 didn’t connect X, Facebook, or TikTok — every one has its own provider (twitter, facebook, tiktok) and its own one-time connect step, identical to the Instagram dance you just did. So adding your X account starts the same way, and the first time it comes back with the same social_account_required — this time pointing at the twitter provider:
curl -X POST https://api.juicer.io/v1/feeds/84213/sources \
-H "Authorization: Bearer $JCR_KEY" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-d '{"platform": "Twitter", "term": "yourbrand", "term_type": "username"}'Open the connection_url from that error, connect the account once, and the retry returns 201. Do the same for Facebook (facebook) and TikTok (tiktok). Each network is a one-time connection; once it's connected, adding more sources for it later is just another POST https://api.juicer.io/v1/feeds/{feed_id}/sources with no interruption.
Want to confirm the lineup? Fetch the feed with its sources embedded:
curl "https://api.juicer.io/v1/feeds/84213?include=sources" \
-H "Authorization: Bearer $JCR_KEY"So far every source we’ve added used term_type: "username" — a specific account. But a source doesn't have to be an account. The same endpoint accepts other term types: pass term_type: "hashtag" to pull every post tagged with a campaign hashtag, or term_type: "channel" with a subreddit name to pull that subreddit. And you can mix them within one feed — a handful of accounts, a campaign hashtag, and a subreddit can all feed the same wall.
One caveat worth being upfront about: how many sources a single feed can hold depends on your plan. The free plan allows one source per feed, so a brand-new key won’t get past the first POST https://api.juicer.io/v1/feeds/{feed_id}/sources — the five-network wall we've been building is really a Starter-plan example (Starter allows five, Pro ten). When you add one past your limit, the API doesn't fail silently; it returns a 422 source_limit_reached with an upgrade link, so it's easy to handle gracefully in a provisioning script. That limit is checked first, too — on the free plan a second source comes back as source_limit_reached before you ever see a connection prompt.
Step 5: get the embed code
The feed exists and is pulling posts. Last step — the snippet you actually put on a website:
curl https://api.juicer.io/v1/feeds/84213/embed \
-H "Authorization: Bearer $JCR_KEY"{
"data": {
"feed_url": "https://www.juicer.io/feeds/our-brand-wall",
"javascript": "<script type=\"text/javascript\" src=\"https://www.juicer.io/embed/our-brand-wall/embed-code.js\" async defer></script>",
"iframe": "<iframe src=\"https://www.juicer.io/api/feeds/our-brand-wall/iframe\" ...></iframe>",
"wordpress_shortcode": "[juicer name='our-brand-wall']",
"wordp
